# Scenes And Shelves

Even a nonfiction book has scenes. A scene is any place where the reader can stand for a moment and understand what is happening.

A shelf is the opposite: a place where ideas are stored for later use. References, notes, appendices, and chapter lists are shelves.

The reader UX needs both. The main column should let scenes breathe, while navigation and metadata should behave like shelves that can be reached without interrupting the room.

If every control is always visible, the page feels busy. If no control is visible, the page feels like a dead document.

The balance is especially important once the manuscript gets long.

Shelves are useful because they imply order without requiring explanation. A frontmatter shelf holds the title page and preface. A chapter shelf holds the main movement of the manuscript. A backmatter shelf holds notes that belong after the ending. The reader does not need to expose those shelves as literal furniture, but it should honor the distinction in the way the book is assembled.

Scenes are useful because they imply duration. A scene can stretch. It can turn. It can pause for an image and then continue. When the mock includes only tiny chapters, the renderer never has to carry a scene through those ordinary changes. When the mock has a few longer chapters, the page has to prove that it can sustain attention.
